title: EHRC Equal Pay Statutory Code of Practice
chapters: Statutory Code of Practice · Issued under Equality Act 2006 s.14 · In effect 6 April 2011 · Covers EqA 2010 Chapter 3 (equality of terms) ss.64–80
desc: The EHRC Equal Pay Statutory Code of Practice provides detailed guidance on the equal pay provisions of the Equality Act 2010 (Chapter 3, sections 64–80). The Code covers the sex equality clause (s.66) which is implied into every employment contract, the three types of equal pay claim (like work under s.65(1), work rated as equivalent under s.65(1)(b), and work of equal value under s.65(1)(c)), the material factor defence (s.69 — the employer must show that the difference in pay is because of a material factor which is not directly or indirectly discriminatory), the role of equal pay audits, the gender pay gap reporting obligations (for employers with 250+ employees), and the relationship between equal pay claims and sex discrimination claims under Part 5 of the Act. The Code explains that equal pay claims are brought using a comparator of the opposite sex doing like work, work rated as equivalent, or work of equal value — this is distinct from the broader sex discrimination provisions. The limitation period for equal pay claims is six years from the date of the last payment at the unequal rate (in England and Wales) or five years (in Scotland).
legalStatus: Statutory Code of Practice. Tribunals must take it into account (Equality Act 2006 s.15(4)).

sections.title: Sex equality clause, comparators, and like work / equal value
sections.summary: The sex equality clause (EqA 2010 s.66) is implied into every employment contract. It provides that if a term in the woman's contract is less favourable than a corresponding term in a man's contract, the woman's term is modified to be no less favourable. The three types of claim: (1) Like work (s.65(1)(a)): the woman and her comparator do work that is the same or broadly similar, and any differences are not of practical importance. (2) Work rated as equivalent (s.65(1)(b)): the woman's job and her comparator's job have been rated as equivalent under a valid job evaluation study. (3) Work of equal value (s.65(1)(c)): the woman's work is of equal value to her comparator's in terms of the demands made on her under headings such as effort, skill, and decision-making. An independent expert may be appointed by the tribunal to assess equal value. The comparator must be employed by the same employer (or an associated employer) at the same establishment or a different establishment where common terms apply.
sections.text:
The sex equality clause (EqA 2010 s.66) operates as follows:

(1) If a term of A's employment is less favourable to A than a corresponding term of B's is to B, A's term is modified so as not to be less favourable.
(2) If A does not have a term corresponding to a term of B's, A's terms are modified so as to include such a term.

Three types of equal pay claim:
• Like work (s.65(1)(a)): work that is the same or broadly similar. Differences must not be of practical importance in relation to the terms and conditions of employment.
• Work rated as equivalent (s.65(1)(b)): a valid analytical job evaluation study has rated the jobs as equivalent. The study must be analytical — it must evaluate the jobs by reference to the demands made under various headings.
• Work of equal value (s.65(1)(c)): work is of equal value in terms of the demands made under headings such as effort, skill, and decision-making. The tribunal may appoint an independent expert.

The material factor defence (s.69): the employer may defeat an equal pay claim by showing that the difference in pay is due to a material factor which is not directly discriminatory, and (if the factor is indirectly discriminatory) which is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

versions.description: Code issued with EqA 2010 commencement. Consolidated previous equal pay guidance from the Equal Opportunities Commission.

leadingCases.name: Hayward v Cammell Laird Shipbuilders Ltd [1988] AC 894
leadingCases.heldText: The House of Lords established the term-by-term comparison approach to equal pay. Each term of the contract must be compared individually — an employer cannot argue that an overall package is equal if individual terms differ. A woman is entitled to the benefit of the most favourable term from the comparator's contract on a term-by-term basis.
leadingCases.significance: Foundation authority on the term-by-term comparison approach — cannot offset less favourable terms against more favourable ones

leadingCases.name: Enderby v Frenchay Health Authority [1993] ECJ Case C-127/92
leadingCases.heldText: The ECJ held that where statistics show a significant disparity in pay between predominantly female and predominantly male groups doing work of equal value, the burden shifts to the employer to justify the difference. The employer must show that the difference is attributable to objectively justified factors unrelated to sex.
leadingCases.significance: Established the statistical evidence approach to establishing a prima facie case in equal pay claims

leadingCases.name: Glasgow City Council v Unison [2017] CSIH 34
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Session considered mass equal pay claims arising from the implementation of a new pay and grading structure. The court confirmed that historical pay practices can create ongoing equal pay liabilities even after a new, non-discriminatory structure is implemented, if the transitional arrangements perpetuate the previous disparity.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that transitional pay protection arrangements can perpetuate equal pay discrimination

refs: EqA 2010 s.66 (sex equality clause — implied into every contract)
EqA 2010 s.71 (sex discrimination in relation to contractual pay — must use equal pay route, not direct discrimination)
Equality Act 2010 (Gender Pay Gap Information) Regulations 2017 (SI 2017/172) — mandatory GPG reporting for 250+ employees
Limitation Act 1980 s.2 (6-year limitation period for equal pay claims in England and Wales)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 s.90 — equality action plans covering gender, ethnicity, disability pay gaps (not yet in force)
ERA 2025 s.97 — pay secrecy clauses unenforceable (not yet in force)
Gender pay gap reporting continues for 250+ employers

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Statutory Code of Practice — approved by resolution of both Houses of Parliament
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Tribunals must take the Code into account (EA 2006 s.15(4)). The Code provides the definitive guidance on the sex equality clause (EqA 2010 s.66), the three types of equal pay claim, the material factor defence, and equal pay audits.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The Equal Pay Code consolidates guidance previously issued by the Equal Opportunities Commission. Equal pay claims have a 6-year limitation period (England and Wales) or 5 years (Scotland), making them among the longest-running employment liabilities. Mass equal pay claims (particularly in local government and NHS) have resulted in settlements exceeding £1 billion.

precedingDocuments.title: Equality Act 2010 Chapter 3 (ss.64-80)
precedingDocuments.significance: The equality of terms provisions. The sex equality clause (s.66) is implied into every employment contract.

precedingDocuments.title: Equal Pay Act 1970 (repealed)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor statute consolidated into EqA 2010 Chapter 3. The case law developed under the EPA 1970 remains applicable.

leadingCases.name: Hayward v Cammell Laird [1988] AC 894
leadingCases.heldText: Term-by-term comparison — cannot offset less favourable terms against more favourable ones.

leadingCases.name: Enderby v Frenchay HA [1993] ECJ C-127/92
leadingCases.heldText: Statistical evidence of pay disparity shifts the burden to the employer to justify.
leadingCases.significance: Statistical evidence approach to prima facie case.

leadingCases.name: Glasgow City Council v Unison [2017] CSIH 34
leadingCases.heldText: Transitional pay arrangements can perpetuate historical equal pay discrimination.
